annotations.label: Even and odd integers

anchor.snippet: An integer is even if it is divisible by 2, and odd if it is not.

annotations.note: Even and Odd are defined as predicates on any additive/multiplicative type, of which ℤ is a special case.

annotations.label: Examples of even and odd numbers

anchor.snippet: For example, −4, 0, and 82 are even numbers, while −3, 5, and 23 are odd numbers.

annotations.note: Numeric examples of specific even/odd integers are not stated as named lemmas in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parity of zero

anchor.snippet: In particular, the parity of zero is even.

annotations.note: Even.zero : Even (0 : α) is registered as a grind_pattern.

annotations.label: Consecutive integers have opposite parity

anchor.snippet: Any two consecutive integers have opposite parity.

annotations.note: even_add_one : Even (a + 1) ↔ Odd a captures the parity flip for consecutive integers.

annotations.label: Parity from last decimal digit

anchor.snippet: An integer expressed in the decimal numeral system is even or odd according to whether its last digit is even or odd.

annotations.note: Nat.digits API exists in Mathlib.Data.Nat.Digits but no lemma directly relates the last decimal digit's parity to n's parity.

annotations.label: Parity in binary and odd bases

anchor.snippet: any integer expressed in the binary numeral system is odd if its last digit is 1; and it is even if its last digit is 0

annotations.note: Binary digits via Nat.bits/digits 2 are defined, and bit/parity correspondences exist, but no single lemma states 'last bit gives parity' as such.

annotations.label: Even number (form 2k)

anchor.snippet: An even number is an integer of the form

annotations.note: Even a := ∃ r, a = r + r in Mathlib, equivalent to ∃ k, a = 2*k.

annotations.label: Even/odd via divisibility by 2

anchor.snippet: An equivalent definition is that an even number is divisible by 2

annotations.note: even_iff_two_dvd : Even a ↔ 2 ∣ a is stated directly.

annotations.label: Set-theoretic definition of even and odd

anchor.snippet: The sets of even and odd numbers can be defined as following

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Even/Odd as predicates rather than as named sets; {n | Even n} can be formed from the predicate.

annotations.label: Parity as ring homomorphism

anchor.snippet: Parity can then be defined as the unique ring homomorphism

annotations.note: The ring hom ℤ → ZMod 2 exists via Int.castRingHom but is not specifically labelled as the 'parity homomorphism'.

annotations.label: Addition/subtraction parity rules

anchor.snippet: even ± even = even

annotations.note: Even.add_odd, Odd.add_even, Odd.add_odd, Even.sub_odd, Odd.sub_even, Odd.sub_odd cover the parity table for ± in Mathlib.Algebra.Ring.Parity.

annotations.label: Multiplication parity rules

anchor.snippet: even × even = even

annotations.note: Even.mul_left, Even.mul_right and Odd.mul together cover the multiplication parity table.

annotations.label: Field with two elements structure

anchor.snippet: the structure ({even, odd}, +, ×) is in fact the field with two elements

annotations.note: ZMod 2 is constructed as a field but Mathlib does not label it as the quotient of ℤ by parity equivalence with an explicit isomorphism to {even, odd}.

annotations.label: Parity of integer quotients

anchor.snippet: when the quotient is an integer, it will be even if and only if the dividend has more factors of two than the divisor

annotations.note: No lemma in Mathlib relates parity of an integer quotient to the 2-adic valuations of dividend and divisor.

annotations.label: Parity of integer coordinate points

anchor.section: Higher dimensions and more general classes of numbers
anchor.snippet: Integer coordinates of points in Euclidean spaces of two or more dimensions also have a parity, usually defined as the parity of the sum of the coordinates.

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a 'parity of an integer-coordinate point' notion.

annotations.label: Mutilated chessboard problem

anchor.section: Higher dimensions and more general classes of numbers
anchor.snippet: This form of parity was famously used to solve the mutilated chessboard problem

annotations.note: No file in Mathlib formalizes the mutilated chessboard problem.

annotations.label: Parity of an ordinal number

anchor.section: Higher dimensions and more general classes of numbers
anchor.snippet: The parity of an ordinal number may be defined to be even if the number is a limit ordinal

annotations.note: Mathlib's Ordinal API has IsLimit but no notion of 'even/odd ordinal'.

annotations.label: Even/odd elements in commutative rings

anchor.section: Higher dimensions and more general classes of numbers
anchor.snippet: Let R be a commutative ring and let I be an ideal of R whose index is 2.

annotations.note: Even/Odd are defined in any (semi)ring but the generalization via an arbitrary index-2 ideal is not given as a definition.

annotations.label: Localization of Z at (2)

anchor.section: Higher dimensions and more general classes of numbers
anchor.snippet: As an example, let R = Z (2) be the localization of Z at the prime ideal (2).

annotations.note: Localization.AtPrime exists generally; the specific instance ℤ_(2) and its parity structure is not singled out.

annotations.label: Even numbers form an ideal

anchor.snippet: The even numbers form an ideal in the ring of integers

annotations.note: The even-integer ideal can be obtained as Ideal.span {2} but Mathlib has no named 'ideal of even integers'.

annotations.label: 2 is the only even prime

anchor.snippet: All prime numbers are odd, with one exception: the prime number 2.

annotations.note: Nat.Prime.even_iff : Even p ↔ p = 2 and Nat.Prime.odd_of_ne_two formalize this statement.

annotations.label: Perfect numbers parity

anchor.snippet: All known perfect numbers are even; it is unknown whether any odd perfect numbers exist.

annotations.note: Perfect numbers are defined and Euler's even-perfect-number theorem is in Archive/Wiedijk100Theorems/PerfectNumbers.lean, but the open problem on odd perfect numbers is not stated.

annotations.label: Goldbach's conjecture

anchor.snippet: Goldbach's conjecture states that every even integer greater than 2 can be represented as a sum of two prime numbers.

annotations.note: Goldbach's conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib (the only 'Goldbach' result is about coprimality of Fermat numbers).

annotations.label: Parity of a permutation

anchor.snippet: The parity of a permutation (as defined in abstract algebra ) is the parity of the number of transpositions into which the permutation can be decomposed.

annotations.note: Equiv.Perm.sign : Perm α →* ℤˣ encodes the parity of a permutation.

annotations.label: Well-definedness of permutation parity

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that no permutation can be decomposed both in an even and in an odd number of transpositions.

annotations.note: Well-definedness is built into Equiv.Perm.sign as a MonoidHom; signAux_eq_signAux2 and related lemmas establish independence of decomposition.

annotations.label: Feit–Thompson theorem

anchor.snippet: The Feit–Thompson theorem states that a finite group is always solvable if its order is an odd number.

annotations.note: Solvable groups are defined (IsSolvable) but the Feit–Thompson theorem itself is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Even and odd functions

anchor.snippet: The parity of a function describes how its values change when its arguments are exchanged with their negations.

annotations.note: Function.Even f := ∀ a, f (-a) = f a and Function.Odd are defined.

annotations.label: Taylor series of even/odd functions

anchor.snippet: The Taylor series of an even function contains only terms whose exponent is an even number

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states that the Taylor coefficients of an even/odd function vanish at odd/even indices respectively.

annotations.label: Evil and odious numbers

anchor.snippet: an evil number is a number that has an even number of 1's in its binary representation , and an odious number is a number that has an odd number of 1's in its binary representation

annotations.note: Evil/odious numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parity function

anchor.snippet: The parity function maps a number to the number of 1's in its binary representation, modulo 2

annotations.note: Mathlib has bit/popcount-style functions but no named 'parity function' returning popcount mod 2.

annotations.label: Thue–Morse sequence

anchor.snippet: The Thue–Morse sequence , an infinite sequence of 0's and 1's, has a 0 in position i when i is evil, and a 1 in that position when i is odious.

annotations.note: The Thue–Morse sequence is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parity bit error detection

anchor.snippet: a parity bit appended to a binary number provides the simplest form of error detecting code

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of parity-bit error detecting codes.

annotations.label: Odd harmonics in cylindrical wind instruments

anchor.snippet: In wind instruments with a cylindrical bore and in effect closed at one end, such as the clarinet at the mouthpiece, the harmonics produced are odd multiples of the fundamental frequency

annotations.note: This is a physics application with no Mathlib formalization.
